skill.name: tdd-workflows-tdd-green
skill.description: Guide an AI coding assistant through the green phase of TDD by implementing the smallest change that makes failing tests pass.
skill.summary: Apply focused TDD green-phase implementation with minimal code, test verification, and refactor notes.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, SQLite, weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and dangerous-combination hits. Manual review found these are Markdown examples and test snippets, not executable skill code, with no evidence of prompt injection or credential exfiltration. The skill still carries medium operational risk because it instructs an AI agent to edit code and run test suites in the user project.

medium_findings.title: Agent May Modify Code and Run Tests
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the assistant to implement minimal passing code and run tests after each change. This is expected for a TDD coding workflow, but it can trigger file writes and project test commands in the target repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly require implementation changes and repeated test execution. The behavior is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it is a real operational risk if used on untrusted projects.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Trigger Command Execution Alerts
low_findings.description: The static command-execution alerts occur in fenced Markdown examples and language labels, not in executable scripts. No shell command, subprocess call, or installer behavior was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown code fences or example test code. They are documentation content and do not execute by themselves.

low_findings.title: Network and Database Alerts Are Local Test Examples
low_findings.description: The network alert is an Express test client call against an application object, and the database alerts are parameterized example queries. No external endpoint, secret transfer, or real database file access was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholder database interfaces and a local test request pattern. I did not find evidence of outbound network calls or credential exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The cited weak-crypto and reconnaissance lines contain skill prose, headings, or harmless examples. No cryptographic API, system probing command, or network discovery behavior was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of representative flagged lines showed ordinary TDD guidance rather than cryptographic or reconnaissance code. This supports dismissing the scanner alerts as pattern noise.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner combined documentation examples into a critical code-execution, network, and credential-access heuristic. Manual review found no executable payload, prompt injection, credential access, or exfiltration path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky elements are separated documentation examples and local testing guidance. I found no semantic evidence that the skill combines them for malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams often overbuild when moving from failing tests to passing tests. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to make the smallest useful implementation, verify tests, and record refactor debt.
actual_capabilities: Reviews failing tests and maps each failure to the smallest implementation need.
Applies green-phase strategies such as fake it, obvious implementation, and triangulation.
Guides repeated test execution after each focused code change.
Keeps implementation scope limited to behavior required by current tests.
Records shortcuts and technical debt for the later refactor phase.
Provides framework-oriented examples for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Django, Express, and React.
limitations: It is not intended for broad refactoring or architecture redesign work.
It depends on existing failing tests that clearly describe required behavior.
It may produce intentionally simple code that needs later cleanup.
It does not replace human review of test commands, generated changes, or project side effects.

use_cases.title: Pass a New Unit Test
use_cases.description: Use the skill after writing a failing test to implement only the behavior that test requires.

use_cases.title: Coach Junior Developers
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to reinforce small increments, test feedback, and separation between green and refactor phases.

use_cases.title: Stabilize AI Coding Sessions
use_cases.description: Use the prompts to keep an AI assistant from adding unrelated features while fixing failing tests.

prompt_templates.title: Start the Green Phase
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner use with one failing test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the TDD green workflow for this failing test. Identify the smallest implementation change, make only that change, run the relevant test, and note any refactor debt.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Several Related Failures
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate use with a small failing suite
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the green phase to these failing tests one at a time. Start with the simplest failure, implement minimal behavior, rerun tests after each change, and stop when the suite is green.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Minimal Strategies
prompt_templates.scenario: Choosing fake it, obvious implementation, or triangulation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these failing tests and choose the green-phase strategy that fits best. Explain the minimal path, implement it, run tests, and list what should wait for refactoring.

prompt_templates.title: Constrain an AI Pairing Session
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced use in an AI coding workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use strict TDD green-phase discipline on this repository. Do not refactor, redesign, add validation, or improve performance unless a failing test requires it. Show test results and refactor notes.

output_examples.input: A failing user creation test expects an id, email, and name.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies a simple in-memory implementation, applies the smallest change, reports the passing test, and records persistence as refactor debt.

output_examples.input: An API endpoint test expects a created task response with status 201.
output_examples.output: The assistant adds a focused handler, verifies the endpoint test, and avoids introducing service layers until refactoring.

output_examples.input: A component test expects a name and email to render.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates the minimal component output, confirms the test passes, and notes styling or state management as later work.

best_practices: Start with the clearest failing test and make one small change at a time.
Run the relevant tests after each implementation step and check for regressions before continuing.
Write down shortcuts, hard-coded values, and missing design work for the refactor phase.
anti_patterns: Adding validation, persistence, caching, or architecture that no failing test requires.
Refactoring while tests are still red or while the needed behavior is unclear.
Changing tests only to make implementation easier instead of satisfying the intended behavior.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it after writing or finding a failing test and before refactoring the implementation.

faq.question: Does it write production-ready code?
faq.answer: It prioritizes passing tests with minimal code. Production hardening belongs in the later refactor phase.

faq.question: Can it run my test suite?
faq.answer: Yes, the workflow asks the assistant to run relevant tests. Review commands before using it on untrusted projects.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written for AI coding assistants, including Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What inputs should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the failing test output, relevant test files, expected behavior, and any project constraints.

faq.question: What should I do after tests pass?
faq.answer: Move to the refactor phase, clean up shortcuts, improve design, and keep the test suite green.
